file_name: Kupriyanov et al. - 2025 - Machine learning–based classification of spatially resolved diffuse reflectance and autofluorescence.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly uses machine learning methods, specifically support vector machine (SVM), discriminant analysis (DA), and multilayer perceptron (MLP), to classify spatially resolved diffuse reflectance and autofluorescence spectra for the diagnosis of skin cancers (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma) and actinic keratoses.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'RESEARCH PAPER' in the header. It presents original experimental data from a clinical study involving 131 patients, describes specific machine learning methods (SVM, DA, MLP) applied to in vivo spectral data, and reports new classification accuracy results for skin cancer diagnostics.

title: Machine learning–based classification of spatially resolved diffuse reflectance and autofluorescence spectra acquired on human skin for actinic keratoses and skin carcinoma diagnostics aid
brief_summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic aid performance of a multimodal optical spectroscopy device for classifying keratinocyte carcinomas and actinic keratoses. The researchers acquired in vivo spatially resolved diffuse reflectance and autofluorescence spectra from 131 patients, covering basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, and healthy skin. They applied support vector machine, discriminant analysis, and multilayer perceptron classifiers in binary and multi-class modes to determine the optimal pipeline. Binary classification accuracies exceeded 80% for distinguishing carcinomas from healthy skin, while actinic keratosis classification ranged from 65% to 75%. The fusion of decision outputs from multiple modalities improved classification accuracy by up to 10 percentage points, demonstrating the benefit of multimodal spectroscopy. The authors concluded that these accuracy levels are promising and comparable to those achieved by general practitioners in skin cancer screening.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four main diagnostic classes studied; n=90 measured areas; also grouped into invasive (CI) and non-invasive (CNI) subtypes.
One of the four main diagnostic classes studied; n=57 measured areas; also grouped into invasive (CI) and non-invasive (CNI) subtypes.
One of the four main diagnostic classes studied; n=96 measured areas; classified as precancerous lesion.
One of the four main diagnostic classes studied; n=608 measured areas; served as the healthy control/comparator class.
Grouped class formed by combining BCC and SCC (or CI and CNI) for specific binary and multi-class comparisons.
Sub-class of carcinomas (BCC/SCC) distinguished by invasiveness status.
Sub-class of carcinomas (BCC/SCC) distinguished by invasiveness status.

all_task_types.target: Histological diagnostic classes (Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Healthy skin) and sub-classes (Invasive vs Non-invasive carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs supervised binary and multi-class classification using Support Vector Machine (SVM), Discriminant Analysis (DA), and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) to predict discrete diagnostic categories from spatially resolved diffuse reflectance and autofluorescence spectra. Tasks include binary classification (e.g., BCC vs H, SCC vs H, AK vs H) and multi-class classification (e.g., H vs AK vs BCC vs SCC, or H vs AK vs CI vs CNI). Accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity are reported as metrics for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: SpectroLive Clinical Dataset
datasets_used.description: A unique and fully open database of spatially resolved diffuse reflectance (DR) and autofluorescence (AF) spectra acquired in vivo using the SpectroLive device from 131 patients at Metz-Thionville Regional Hospital (France) between 2017 and 2021. The dataset includes measurements from lesions, peri-lesion areas, and non-lesion healthy skin, with ground truth provided by histopathological analysis.

use.training: Used for training support vector machine (SVM), discriminant analysis (DA), and multilayer perceptron (MLP) classifiers for binary and multi-class classification tasks.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter optimization and selection of the best classification pipeline (normalization, feature extraction, and classifier parameters).
use.testing: Used for evaluating classification performance (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) of the developed models.
use.other: The dataset is described as 'fully open' and was previously published in Ref. 53.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance and select the best-performing classifier and hyperparameters based on average final classification accuracy.
To obtain statistically reliable results by repeating the sequence of random sample selection five times.
To avoid the influence of different numbers of samples per class on the final classification accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.method: Outlier filtering using Coefficient of Variation (CV)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To identify and exclude measurements with significant differences between spectra, using a threshold of 0.08 for trios and pairs of spectra.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Statistical reliability was addressed by repeating the random sample selection sequence five times to obtain statistically reliable results, and class balancing was used to avoid influence of sample size differences.

BCC vs H.notes: Fusion of all modalities and SDS provided highest Acc (87%) with improved Se (78%) and high Sp (95%).

SCC vs H.notes: Fusion step allowed improvement of Se and Acc by 7 and 2 percentage points respectively compared with single modalities and SDS while maintaining high Sp of 95%.

AK vs H.notes: Classification achieved a 65% to 75% accuracy for AK versus other classes. Fusion provided balanced Se, Sp, and Acc.

H vs KC (BCC + SCC).notes: Overall single modality accuracy decreased to 79% in best case. After applying decision fusion, classification accuracy increased to 83%.

BCC vs SCC.notes: Classification accuracy was one of the lowest for discriminating BCC from SCC (between 55% and 70% for all SDS and light source combinations).

H vs Rest (BCC, SCC, AK).notes: High Sp of 91% obtained with limited Acc (75%) due to low Se of 58%. Fusion provided most balanced model.

AK vs KC (BCC + SCC).notes: Highest accuracy value (78%) obtained after fusion (+9 pp compared with highest Acc for single modality).

general_findings.notes: In multiclass (three or four classes) classification modes, accuracy reached 57%. Fusion of decisions increased classification accuracies (up to 10 percentage point-increase). Classification accuracy is between 75% and 80% when AK is included, whereas it is above 83% when AK is not included.

architecture_used.main_model: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Discriminant Analysis (DA) [specifically Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)], and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)
architecture_used.input_size: Spatially resolved diffuse reflectance (DR) and autofluorescence (AF) spectra; specific input dimensions not reported, but feature extraction reduced dimensionality (PCA components: 15-20; Autoencoder hidden layers: 80, 40, 80)

SVM.kernel: Linear, polynomial (degree 2 to 3), radial basis function (RBF)

training_details.optimization_method: Hyperparameter optimization based on average final classification accuracy from cross-validation
training_details.validation_strategy: Repeated random sampling cross-validation (sequence repeated 5 times)
training_details.hardware: PC Intel Core i7-11850H, RAM 32Gb, Windows 11
training_details.training_time: A few dozen seconds to a few minutes

fusion_methods: Stacking (using SVM or Random Forest with 80 trees as meta-classifier)

preprocessing_techniques: Median filtering (window size = 3) to suppress impulse noise
Smoothing using Savitzky–Golay filter (window size = 81, polynomial order = 4)
Resampling from 0.3 nm step to 1 nm step
Intensity correction of spectral response for autofluorescence detection channels
Outlier detection and removal based on average coefficients of variation (threshold = 0.08) for trios and pairs of spectra
Maximum intensity normalization (AF: 420-600 nm; DR: 630-690 nm)
Ratio normalization using healthy skin reference spectra
conclusions: Binary classification accuracies for discriminating basal cell carcinoma (BCC) or squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) from healthy skin exceeded 80%.
Classification accuracy for actinic keratosis (AK) versus other classes ranged from 65% to 75%.
In multiclass classification modes involving three or four classes, accuracy reached 57%.

Fusion of decision outputs from multiple modalities increased classification accuracies by up to 10 percentage points, demonstrating the benefit of multimodal spectroscopy over single modality approaches.
The observed levels of classification accuracy are promising and comparable to those obtained by general practitioners in keratinocyte carcinoma screening.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a single-center dataset (Metz-Thionville Regional Hospital, France) collected between 2017 and 2021, which may introduce geographic or demographic bias and limits generalizability without external validation.

Actinic keratosis (AK) classification is notably weak (65%–75% accuracy), attributed to its histological overlap with both healthy skin and squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), creating a confusing training signal for the classifier.

Discriminating between Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) yielded low accuracy (55%–70%), indicating the spectroscopic features are insufficient for reliable subtype differentiation.
Multiclass classification performance drops significantly (50%–59% accuracy) compared to binary tasks, highlighting the difficulty of distinguishing four distinct classes simultaneously.
The dataset is heavily imbalanced, with 608 healthy skin samples compared to 90 BCC, 57 SCC, and 96 AK samples; class balancing was required to prevent bias toward the healthy class.
No formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; statistical reliability was addressed only by repeating random sampling sequences five times.

The device and dataset are described as 'fully open' and previously published, which is a strength for reproducibility, but the clinical applicability is constrained by the need for specialized optical equipment.
The study compares ML performance to general practitioners' accuracy (54%–88%) rather than dermatologists, which may overestimate the relative advantage of the tool in expert settings.
Real-time classification capability is claimed (few seconds per spectrum), but the software integration was still under development at the time of publication, representing a deployment gap.
The study does not address inter-observer variability in the ground truth histopathological labels, which could affect the reliability of the supervised learning targets.
